sentences.text: Hungary.
Hungary.
It's confusing because it changed.
Anyway, we made that dash after we heard the-- the echo.
The barking of the dogs got weaker and weaker.
So we were sure that the patrol is far away.
And we dashed across the border.
And the other side of the border is-- is grassy.
It was not-- not-- not-- forested with-- with trees.
And about-- running about 5, 10 minutes on the other side of the Beskids, my brother Tsvi said to me, I-- I know where we are.
He recognized-- and that was a great, great relief for me that at least we know where-- recognized our--
So we-- we started going.
And our aim was from the Beskids to reach a mountain called [INAUDIBLE].
The [INAUDIBLE] Mountain was a lower one.
And it was not-- not forested.
And that one, the [INAUDIBLE] Mountain, from the right side lay Torun.
From the left side lay [?
Lapszanka, ?] which was part of-- part of it.
And from both sides, there are two-- two rivers that merge at the end into one.
And our-- our forest that we owned was on the left side.
The house where we lived was on the right side.
We could see from the top of the mountain, around 6:00, 7:00, we could see that the lights were on in the house.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] After we walked for maybe an hour, all of a sudden we noticed somebody lighting a cigarette.
And we got so scared that this is another patrol.
So I started pulling my brother Tsvi.
I started pulling him down.
And the other two brothers were together.
In this great scare, we ran down to the right, and those two guys ran-- ran away on the other side.
And it took-- I don't know how long-- a few minutes.
And we started climbing back.
But we were-- we were afraid to yell to him, where are the Prisent brothers, [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE]?
Where are they?
I'll make it short.
We got split up that night on top of that mountain.
And we never saw each other.
After the war, in 1948, I-- I found in a camp in Germany-- the older brother survived.
And the younger one did not survive.
But we didn't see each other from that year, '41, till 1948.
Anyway, we got lost.
We-- we got lost.
And so we started climbing back.
And then we came against a fence.
And then we saw another light, cigarette, somebody-- somebody started.
And a guy is approaching us.
But we saw already it's one guy, not two.
Usually patrols are in two.
So-- and he-- he yelled out, who is there?
[NON-ENGLISH] in Ukrainian.
My brother answered.
He said-- he didn't tell him who we are.
He said, we lost our way here.
We-- we are-- we want to go to the [INAUDIBLE], to the way to the [INAUDIBLE], to the name of the mountain.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he said, oh, you-- you are-- you are refugees, yes?
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he said, you go from here up to the right and-- and then make another right.
That-- that will be the [INAUDIBLE] on top of it.
And I have to mention here what he said.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He said in Ukrainian, in Russian, [NON-ENGLISH] Go with God's help where God has directed you.
Goodbye.
When he said those words, my brother recognized his voice.
He knew who he was.
We were afraid that he recognized my brother's voice, but we didn't know.
But anyway, he-- he was the one, actually, that was lighting the cigarette before.
And we got-- we got all-- all scared because he saw people at night going around there.
So we followed the way, the directions the way he told us, but the two brothers we lost, and we couldn't find them anymore.
And we started going a little further, and then-- my father owned a piece of land on that mountain, [INAUDIBLE].
And I even remembered at one time that he came and he showed me the border of this-- this-- where that piece of land-- where it borders.
So I recognized already myself.
We new where we are.
So that was a relief.
But when looking down, we knew that our house is across the street from the Hebrew day school.
And when we left, we knew that the command, the Hungarian command, is in the school.
So we were-- we decided we will not go to our house.
Instead, we knew that on the left side at the end of this mountain, which is [?
Lapszanka. ?] And that-- on that place, there is one of the 18 Jews that were left.
The older one, his name was Neuman, Dudie.
We will, instead, go and see if he is there.
But we won't go to our own house.
And--
Well, when I started telling the story-- when they surrounded the 135 families, about 800 Jews in Torun, they left 17 or 18 people-- they left in Torun.
They did not-- they did not deport them.
Among them, among the 17 or 18, was my mother and my father.
But I knew that this one, Dudie, he was also the same-- the same age as my father, a little older, that he was-- so we decided instead to go to his place than to our place.
Because we knew that there is definitely police around there, gendarmes, whatnot.
We came down that mountain.
At the still of the night on top of the mountain, we could even hear our-- our lumber mill working.
I remember my brother Tsvi asking me, do you hear the-- our mill working?
It was working.
That was five months later-- four months, from August 2 till-- that-- that was the end of October.
Anyway, when we came to-- to this house, to that Dudie Neuman, we came around the back.
And we knocked at the back window.
It was dark.
And that day had to be-- it had to be, I think, on a Sunday.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
And they were afraid to open up the window.
They didn't open up.
And I-- I knew I started banging hard.
I said, Dudie, I said, Mr.
Neuman, this is Abraham's children.
Tsvi and Michael is here.
When he heard what we were talking about, he opened the window.
And he said to us, oh my God, children, what have you done?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
If somebody would plunge a knife into my chest, it wouldn't hurt any worse than to get-- oh my God, children, what have you done?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We couldn't-- we couldn't believe.
He knew our father.
He knew our family, and-- and-- what he said-- we couldn't understand it.
We were absolutely numbed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So, the reason why he said that was-- we didn't know that at the time.
We found that out a few weeks or months later.
His daughter, that was in her third, fourth month of pregnancy when they rounded everybody up, was taken out with her husband.
And they managed to came across like we did.
And she-- straight when she was already at that time in the eighth or ninth month of pregnancy, and she came back.
And where would she go, a pregnant woman?
She went to her parents, her parents' house.
The Hungarian gendarmes came into the house and dragged her out from her parents' house and killed her.
The next day, they found her dead.
And her husband somehow escaped.
And-- and here, this happened only a few-- couple of weeks before we came.
But we didn't know about it.
And that's why this man said, oh my God, children, what have you done?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Finally-- finally-- he settled down a little bit.
And he said to us, I can't let you in here.
He said-- first of all we asked him, is our father home, our mother?
He said, yes, they are home.
But I can't let you in.
I'll tell you what you do.
Not too far from there was a mikvah A mikvah was a public place.
You know what a mikvah is.
You go in there and get on-- get on top of the attic.
There is hay there.
So you can lay down there.
There's a ladder.
You can-- you can-- but we cannot take you in.
You cannot stay here in case they come and they find you here.
If they find you over there, then nobody's responsible.
It's your own life, and that's-- and-- and that's it.
But in meantime, he said he had some challah.
That's why I think it must have been either shabbat or Sunday.
Because he gave us a challah that was left over from shabbat.
And he says, you take this with you so you have something to eat.
And I will go over tomorrow and notify your dad and mom.
And you stay there, and we will get in touch with you.
But when we went away from there, we did find that mikvah, and we did climb up on the attic.
But we couldn't understand what happened.
How is it possible that a person that we lived together here-- we lived in-- and that he-- is it-- is it that dangerous that they wouldn't let us in?
We didn't know what happened to him, that his own daughter was dragged out and killed.
So we got up.
We got up on that attic.
We found some straw there.
And-- and of course, we couldn't sleep.
We couldn't sleep from the great shock that we had.
And we didn't know-- we didn't know what to do.
We are at the mercy to wait.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
We waited the next day.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] also, not too far from that mikvah, in that place, in that village [?
Lapszanka, ?] was a family by the name of Goddinger.
And the man was a tailor.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was the only tailor in this whole community.
And the mayor from-- from this [?
Lapszanka ?] community insisted that when they rounded up that the Hungarians leave him because they don't have a tailor.
They need a tailor.
So they left his family.
His son was in a labor camp.
But his daughter was home.
And his daughter was one that I went to school with.
So I had a friend-- we were a little bit more than friendly with his-- her name was Tova.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Next night-- we could barely wait till next night.
Next night, we heard quietly that the door of that mikvah opened up.
We couldn't see.
It was pitch-black dark.
This girl Tova came in.
And she-- she brought us-- she brought us food.
She brought us wurst and bread, and some sodas to drink.
And she told us that Dudie-- that Neuman went and talked to our parents, that our father instructed us that we should stay there.
He is going to travel the next day to the-- to a village, which was the county-- head of the county called [?
Wolow. ?] He will-- he will-- and he will try to do something to-- to get us out of here.
Because we cannot stay in the Carpathian anymore.
We have to get deep inside Hungary.
So-- and she asked us what-- if we need anything, what.
She will come tomorrow and bring it to us.
So we told her the first thing we need is-- is a watch-- because we didn't have anymore-- so that we know the time.
And, if possible, she should bring us cigarettes.
Under the great-- we were both smokers at that time.
Under the great tension, without-- without cigarettes it was--
So-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and we have to wait till further instructions.
So we stayed on that attic [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] three days-- three days, four nights and three days.
The next day, my father managed to go to get to the city [?
Wolow. ?] That was the head-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] from the area that was the-- no, what do you say?
Not the capital-- the county seat-- the county seat.
And for a lot of money-- I don't remember the amount-- he was able to hire a Hungarian detective that had a car.
And he would come for us to a certain place and take us from there into the city of [INAUDIBLE].
But we have to wait for instructions.
Until then, the next-- the next day this girl-- this girl brought us some cigarettes.
She brought us a watch.
And she-- well, we had already-- we had already food.
On the third or fourth day, we-- we got a letter, a written instructions from our father.
We had to wait till we were able to read that, you know.
And the instructions were that he hired that detective.
And this detective will come the next day around 11:00 or 10:00.
I don't remember exactly the time.
I have it here written.
From the city of [INAUDIBLE], there were kilometer stones on the highway.
To the border it was 87 kilometers.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And those stones were painted white.
And it said, 72 kilometers, 73, 74.
Every kilometer has a stone.
And the instruction was that we should go between the two villages, between Torun and Bystra, which is four or five kilometers.
There is the 78-kilometer stone there.
There will come a car from this direction and will turn around by this kilometer stone.
And he will turn off the lights two times, turn it on and turn it off.
That will-- that will be the sign.
We will know that this is the man that will take us.
Then we will ascend the mountain.
I forgot to say what is said-- what we had to do.

We had to get out from this mikvah place, cross a river into another forest that was called [INAUDIBLE], go two and a half kilometers inside the forest, then recross the river again and cross the highway and go into the other side of the highway.
That forest is called [INAUDIBLE].
And we are to be on top there.
From there, we can see any car coming.
When the car will come, then we descend down there.
We get in.
We did remember that crossing that-- that river, that there was a small [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] pedestrian bridge.
A little-- a few hundred meters below, there was a regular bridge that-- that wagons and trucks could go.
But we didn't want to cross it with that bridge.
Fortunately, we remembered that there is a small-- a small that what's called a [NON-ENGLISH].
We will go over there, and we came, and we found it.
It was still there.
And we managed to-- to get across.
And the way the instructions were, we met on that side of the mountain two kilometers down.
We knew the area well.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And so we came to that place.
And we sat there.
And we waited for that-- for that to come.
That detective came in time.
And we-- we saw he turned around, and he flashed-- turned the lights off and on.
And we descended the mountain.
And we got into the car.
And we had 20 kilometers to the-- to the-- to [?
Wolow. ?] That place had to be-- it was a dangerous point to cross.
Because [?
Wolow ?] was only 25 kilometers from the border.
And over there, every car, every truck, was-- was checkpoints.
There was a checkpoint that we had to cross.
We got into the car.
Our-- our-- our bad luck turned out that a few minutes later he had a flat tire.
And the Hungarians are noted for-- for very, very good curses.
So he started cursing in Hungarian, and, get out of the car!
So we got out of the car.
So fortunately, he was able to change his flat in a few minutes.
And nobody-- nobody came or saw us.
We got in.
And then he came in, and he brought us into this place in [?
Wolow. ?] And he said he has to go out and get gasoline in-- in-- in there.
And he said, it's only going to take a few minutes, but you lay down on the floor.
And he covered us up in the back of the car with-- with some kind of a cover that you shouldn't see that there are people in there.
And you can imagine-- my brother was also six-foot tall.
And the two of us-- how we curled up in the back like worms, I'll never know.
But we were there, and it took this guy a long time.
It took him more than 10 minutes.
And we waited and waited and waited.
Finally he came.
And he said, now you stay covered like you are because we are going to cross a checkpoint here, and I don't want to have to see you sitting.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that took another 15, 20 minutes till we went outside of [?
Wolow. ?] And fortunately they didn't stop him.
And we passed.
Finally, he said, you can-- you can get off and sit down in the car.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
We-- we managed to get to the city of [INAUDIBLE].
He was supposed to take us to a certain family, but he didn't.
He brought us into another family.
And he dumped us off over there.
And-- and he turned around, and he just knocked at the window.
He said, I brought two Jews from Torun.
And that was it.
That family-- I didn't know the family, but my brother Tsvi told me that he knows that family.
OK?
